title: DNS Record Types
description:
## Pointing a Name at Another Name

DNS maps names to data using typed records: **A** (IPv4 address), **AAAA** (IPv6 address), **CNAME** (alias to another name), **MX** (mail exchange), **TXT** (arbitrary text, e.g. SPF/verification).

You want **`shop.example.com`** to resolve to whatever **`store-platform.vendor.net`** resolves to, so that if the vendor changes their IP you don't have to update anything. Which record type should you create?

options.text: An **A** record pointing `shop.example.com` at the vendor's current IPv4 address.
A **CNAME** record making `shop.example.com` an alias of `store-platform.vendor.net`.

explanation: A **CNAME** (Canonical Name) record makes one hostname an **alias** of another hostname. Resolving `shop.example.com` then follows the alias to `store-platform.vendor.net` and uses *its* address — so if the vendor changes their IP, you never touch your record. An **A** record (a) hard-codes a specific IPv4 address, which breaks the moment the vendor's IP changes (that's exactly what you're avoiding). **MX** records (c) only direct email delivery, and **TXT** records (d) hold free-form text (like SPF policies) and are not used for resolving a host to an address. (Note: A/AAAA differ only in IP version — A is IPv4, AAAA is IPv6.)

hints.title: Alias vs fixed address
hints.body: You want to point at a *name*, not a number, so that the underlying IP can change without your involvement. Which record type aliases one name to another?
